title: Circles
summary: Chapter 10 explores the relationship between a circle and lines. A tangent is a line that touches a circle at exactly one point, and the chapter proves that the tangent at any point is perpendicular to the radius at that point. Students learn that two tangents drawn from an external point are equal in length. These properties are applied to solve problems involving tangent lengths, angles, and combinations of circles and triangles.
learningPoints: Define tangent to a circle as a line touching the circle at exactly one point
State and prove that the tangent at any point is perpendicular to the radius through that point
Prove that exactly two tangents can be drawn from an external point
Prove that tangents drawn from an external point are equal in length
Apply the properties of tangents to find unknown lengths
Find the angle between two tangents drawn from an external point
Apply properties of tangents in combination with triangles and quadrilaterals
Prove that opposite sides of a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle are equal in sum
Solve problems using the relationship between tangent and chord
Apply Pythagoras theorem with tangent-radius problems

formulas.label: Tangent-Radius Perpendicularity
formulas.description: The radius OT to the point of tangency is perpendicular to the tangent PT.

formulas.label: Equal Tangents from External Point
formulas.description: If PA and PB are tangents from external point P to a circle, then PA = PB.

formulas.label: Angle Between Tangents
formulas.description: The angle between two tangents from an external point and the angle at the centre are supplementary.

formulas.label: Tangent Length Formula
formulas.description: Length of tangent from external point P to circle with centre O and radius r.

formulas.label: Circumscribed Quadrilateral
formulas.description: For a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle, the sum of opposite sides are equal.

resources.label: Circles — Video Lessons
resources.description: Videos on tangents, chord properties and circle theorems.

mcqs.question: A tangent to a circle intersects it in:
mcqs.explanation: A tangent touches the circle at exactly one point — the point of tangency.

mcqs.question: From a point 13cm from centre of circle of radius 5cm, length of tangent is:
The angle between a tangent and radius at point of contact is:
mcqs.explanation: The tangent is always perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency.

mcqs.question: Two tangents PA and PB from external point P have PA = 8cm. Then PB equals:
mcqs.explanation: Tangents from an external point are always equal. So PB = PA = 8cm.

mcqs.question: If TP and TQ are tangents from T and ∠PTQ = 70°, then ∠POQ is:
If PA and PB are tangents with ∠APB = 80°, then ∠OAP is:
mcqs.explanation: OAP is right angle. Triangle OAP: ∠AOP = (180-80)/2 = 50°. ∠OAP = 180-90-50 = 40°.

mcqs.question: A quadrilateral ABCD is circumscribed about a circle. If AB=8, BC=7, CD=6, then DA is:
mcqs.explanation: AB + CD = BC + DA. 8 + 6 = 7 + DA. DA = 7.

mcqs.question: The number of tangents that can be drawn from an external point is:
mcqs.explanation: Exactly two tangents can be drawn from any external point to a circle.

mcqs.question: In a right triangle ABC with right angle at B, a circle is inscribed. If AB=6, BC=8, the radius is:
A point P is 26cm from centre. Tangent length is 24cm. Radius of circle is:
The line segment joining two points on a circle is called:
mcqs.explanation: A chord is a line segment with both endpoints on the circle.

mcqs.question: How many tangents can be drawn to a circle from a point on the circle?
mcqs.explanation: Exactly one tangent can be drawn from a point on the circle.

mcqs.question: PA is tangent at A, PBC is secant. If PA=4, PB=2, then PC is:
If two circles of radii 5 and 3 are externally tangent, distance between centres is:
mcqs.explanation: For external tangency, distance = r₁ + r₂ = 5 + 3 = 8.

mcqs.question: Triangle ABC is circumscribed about a circle. If AB=8, BC=9, CA=7, tangent from A is:
mcqs.explanation: Let tangents from A,B,C be x,y,z. x+y=8, y+z=9, z+x=7. Adding: 2(x+y+z)=24, x+y+z=12. x=12-9=3.

exercises.question: Prove that the tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
exercises.answer: Let O be centre, P point on circle, PT be tangent. Take any point Q on tangent. OQ > OP (as Q is outside circle). This is true for all points except P. So OP is shortest distance from O to tangent line. Therefore OP ⊥ PT.

exercises.question: Two concentric circles of radii 5cm and 3cm. Find length of chord of outer circle touching inner circle.
exercises.answer: The chord of outer circle is tangent to inner circle. Let chord = AB, tangent point = P. OP ⊥ AB where O is centre. OP = 3 (radius of inner), OA = 5. AP = √(25-9) = √16 = 4. AB = 2×4 = 8 cm.

exercises.question: Prove that tangents drawn from an external point are equal.
exercises.answer: Let PA and PB be tangents from P to circle with centre O. In triangles OAP and OBP: OA=OB (radii), OP=OP (common), ∠OAP=∠OBP=90° (tangent⊥radius). By RHS, triangles are congruent. Therefore PA=PB.

exercises.question: From a point 17cm from centre, tangents are drawn to circle of radius 8cm. Find length of tangent.
AB is a chord of circle with centre O. C is a point on major arc. If ∠OAB = 35°, find ∠ACB.
exercises.answer: OA=OB (radii) so ∠OAB=∠OBA=35°. ∠AOB=180-70=110°. Angle at circumference = half central angle = 55°. ∠ACB = 55°.

exercises.question: Two tangents TP and TQ from external point T. Show that ∠PTQ = 2∠OPQ.
exercises.answer: Since TP=TQ (equal tangents), triangle TPQ is isosceles. ∠TPQ=∠TQP. Let ∠OPT=90°. ∠OPQ = 90°-∠TPQ. ∠PTQ = 180°-2∠TPQ = 2(90°-∠TPQ) = 2∠OPQ. Proved.

exercises.question: A triangle ABC is drawn to circumscribe a circle of radius 4cm. BC=6, BD=8 where D is tangent point on BC. Find AB and AC.
exercises.answer: Let tangent lengths from A,B,C be x, BD=8, DC=6-... wait BC=6, BD=8 means DC=BC-BD. Recalculate: Let tangent from C = a, from B = 8, from A = x. BC = BD+DC = 8+a = 6? Contradiction. Let DC = 3 (since BC=6, BD=3). Tangent from C=3, from B=3. s = (AB+BC+CA)/2. Area = r×s. Solve for AB and AC.

exercises.question: Prove that opposite sides of a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle are supplementary in their sums.
exercises.answer: Let ABCD circumscribe circle. Let tangent lengths from A,B,C,D be p,q,r,s. AB = p+q, BC = q+r, CD = r+s, DA = s+p. AB+CD = p+q+r+s = BC+DA. Therefore AB+CD = BC+DA. Proved.
